title: Essay on Shinylive
text: Assume that my coworkers are interested in learning about Shinylive. Explain what Shinylive is, how it differs from traditional Shiny apps, and the pros and cons of using Shinylive compared with Shiny.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text: Assume that my coworkers are interested in learning about Shinylive. Explain what Shinylive is, how it differs from traditional Shiny apps, and the pros and cons of using Shinylive compared with Shiny.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
What Is Shinylive?
Shinylive is a way to run Shiny apps directly in the user’s web browser. A traditional Shiny app usually needs a server because the app logic runs outside the user’s computer. Shinylive is different because it allows certain Shiny apps to run in the browser instead.
In simpler terms, Shinylive makes it possible to publish some interactive Shiny examples without needing a traditional Shiny server. This can be helpful for class projects, tutorials, demonstrations, and websites made with Quarto.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Traditional Shiny Apps vs. Shinylive
The biggest difference between traditional Shiny and Shinylive is where the app runs. In a traditional Shiny app, the user opens the app in a browser, but the server handles the calculations and sends the results back. With Shinylive, the app runs locally inside the browser.
This difference matters because it changes how the app is published and maintained. Traditional Shiny apps are powerful, but they usually require server hosting. Shinylive can be easier to share on static websites because it does not need the same server setup.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Pros of Shinylive
One advantage of Shinylive is that it can make publishing easier. Since the app can run in the browser, it can be used in static websites and learning materials without the same kind of server setup. This makes it useful for smaller apps or examples where the goal is to demonstrate an idea.
Another advantage is that Shinylive fits well with Quarto websites. Since this assignment focuses on creating and publishing a website, Shinylive is helpful because it supports interactivity while still keeping the website easier to share.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Cons of Shinylive
One limitation of Shinylive is that it may not be the best option for large or complex apps. Since the app runs in the browser, performance can depend on the user’s computer and browser. If the app has large data, private data, or heavy calculations, traditional Shiny may be a better choice.
Another limitation is that Shinylive may not support every package or workflow in the same way as a server-based Shiny app. This means the developer needs to test the app carefully before deciding to use Shinylive.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
When I Would Use Shinylive
I would use Shinylive for smaller examples, classroom demonstrations, tutorials, and website-based apps. For example, if I wanted to show coworkers how changing a slider affects a chart, Shinylive would be a good option because the example could run directly in the website.
However, if I were creating a larger business dashboard with private company data or more advanced calculations, I would probably use a traditional Shiny app instead. That type of app would need more control over performance, hosting, and security.

title: Essay on Shinylive
text:
Reflection
Shinylive helped me understand that interactive apps do not always have to be hosted the same way. Traditional Shiny apps are powerful, but they usually need a server. Shinylive is useful because it makes some Shiny apps easier to share through websites. I think the best choice depends on the size of the app, the type of data, and how the audience will use it.

title: Home
text:
Welcome to my Module 10 website. This website highlights what I learned throughout this module about Quarto websites, dashboards, Shiny apps, and Shinylive. As I worked through the videos and activities, I learned how these tools can be used to create more interactive and organized ways to present information. Each page focuses on a different topic from the module and shows what I practiced along the way.


My name is Dia Kaewdee, and I’m currently working toward my bachelor’s degree in Marketing. I enjoy learning about consumer behavior, branding, and how businesses use data to make decisions. This course has also helped me become more comfortable using R and Quarto to organize information in a way that is easy to understand and visually appealing.



My career goal is to work in a creative marketing or fashion-related role where I can combine creativity with business strategy. I’m especially interested in buying, product development, branding, and consumer insights because they allow me to use both analytical and creative thinking. I hope to continue building technical skills like R and data visualization while developing my marketing knowledge.

The purpose of this website is to showcase what I learned in Module 10. The dashboard page shows how data can be presented visually. The Shiny Apps page explains how traditional Shiny apps work. The Shinylive page explains how serverless Shiny apps are different from traditional Shiny apps.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text: Assume that my coworkers are interested in learning about Shiny apps and I am helping teach them. Create lecture notes that explain what Shiny is, how it works, and why it is useful.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text: Assume that my coworkers are interested in learning about Shiny apps and I am helping teach them. Create lecture notes that explain what Shiny is, how it works, and why it is useful.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
What Is Shiny?
Shiny is a tool that allows people to build interactive web applications using R. Instead of only creating a static report, a Shiny app allows users to interact with the information. For example, the user might choose an option from a dropdown menu, move a slider, or click a button. After the user makes a choice, the chart, table, or other output can update automatically.
The main idea behind Shiny is that it connects user input with output. This makes it useful for dashboards, business tools, calculators, and data exploration. In a regular report, the author decides what the viewer sees. In a Shiny app, the viewer can explore the data more directly.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
section: Main Parts of a Shiny App
text:
Main Parts of a Shiny App
A traditional Shiny app usually has two main parts: the user interface and the server.
The user interface is the part the viewer sees. This can include the app title, text, sliders, dropdown menus, buttons, tables, and charts. In simple terms, this part controls the layout and the appearance of the app.
The server is the part that controls the app logic. It tells the app what to do when the user changes an input. For example, if the user selects a different vehicle class, the server can update the chart to only show cars from that class.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Why Shiny Is Useful
Shiny is useful because it makes data more interactive and flexible. In a business setting, different coworkers may care about different parts of the same data. A manager may want to view sales by region, while a marketing team may want to view customer behavior by segment. A Shiny app can allow both groups to use the same tool while choosing the information that matters most to them.
Another reason Shiny is useful is that it helps make data communication more active. Instead of only reading results, the user can test different options and see how the output changes.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Strengths of Shiny Apps
One strength of Shiny is that it works well with R, which is already used for data cleaning, analysis, and visualization. This means the same general workflow can be used to analyze data and build an app.
Another strength is that Shiny apps can make complicated information easier to understand. When users can interact with the information, they may be able to answer their own questions more easily.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Limitations of Shiny Apps
One limitation of traditional Shiny apps is that they usually need a server to run. This can make publishing and maintaining the app more complicated than publishing a regular HTML page. Another limitation is that large apps may become slower if the data is large or if many people are using the app at the same time.

title: Essay on Shiny Apps
text:
Reflection
Overall, I see Shiny as a way to turn a regular data report into an interactive tool. This is helpful because the audience does not have to rely only on one version of the results. They can use the app to explore the information in a way that connects to their own question.
